title: Lifted veils: Algerian doctoral students' experiences and changing perceptions of study abroad and intercultural competence in UK higher education
author: Abdessalam, Amina
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: With the continuous growth of internationalisation, there is an evolving interest in experiential learning and students’ mobility within higher education. Given this and while it has been increasingly important to gain an understanding to the benefits and influence of study abroad on learner’s competencies and skills through research, there is still a need to look at the phenomena starting from the students’ own experiences. Within the existing research, the focus is on where student mobility is well established, and there is less research on the experience of students from unconventional settings such as Algeria. Few recent studies have explored the experience of Algerians students in the UK and there is no research that focuses on the study abroad lived experience of Algerian students and their understanding of intercultural competence. Thus, this is the focus of this study. Methodologically, this research takes a non-traditional path using a phenomenological autoethnography approach. I conducted a two-part study, firstly from the perspective of six students from a range of UK Higher Education institutions and secondly from the perspective of myself as a student (evocative autoethnography). My autoethnography highlights a dialogue between myself, the other students and the reviewed relevant literature. This research intends to seek meaning through unveiling the experiences of study abroad and intercultural competence through students’ articulated accounts and my own reflective account. To present findings I crafted narratives from interviews with students and contrasted them to those of my own to explore what remained unsaid in my participants’ accounts. It became evident that intergenerational trauma from Algeria’s recent history is an important contextual factor which features within participants’ experiences. In comparing narratives, I draw out the gender dynamics at play within the succession of decisions that study abroad entails for Algerian students, from initial financial arrangements through negotiation of supervision to topic and research development. Moreover, the importance of both formal and informal experiences, particularly as concerns students’ changing perception of interaction with those who are culturally different than them, is examined. Challenges such as isolation, loneliness, islamophobia and uncertainty were experienced differently by students with gender being a crucial factor. The hope is that this study will open a new door for further work on the gendered dimensions of student mobility and internationalisation of Higher Education in relation to the MENA region.
